content:
# Ruth Chapter 4 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Book:** Ruth | **Chapter:** 4

---

### Verse 1 -- peloni almoni: "so-and-so" vs. "friend"
- **EMET:** "'Turn-aside, sit-down here, so-and-so' [peloni almoni]"
- **LOGOS:** "'Turn aside, sit down here, So-and-so'"
- **PNEUMA:** "'Come over here, friend -- sit down'"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts a hybrid: "Turn aside, sit down here, friend." The Hebrew peloni almoni means "so-and-so" -- the narrator's way of withholding the name. EMET and LOGOS render the idiom literally, which reads strangely as direct address. PNEUMA's "friend" provides a plausible form of address. SOPHIA retains EMET/LOGOS's "Turn aside" (Hebrew surah) over PNEUMA's "Come over here."

### Verse 3 -- "has sold" vs. "is selling"
- **EMET:** "has-sold Naomi" (Hebrew perfect tense)
- **LOGOS:** "Naomi... is selling it"
- **PNEUMA:** "Naomi... is selling the piece of land"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "is selling." The perfect tense makherah likely functions as a performative or imminent-action perfect in this legal context.

### Verse 3 -- "our brother" vs. "our relative"
- **EMET:** "to-our-brother"
- **LOGOS:** "our brother Elimelech"
- **PNEUMA:** "our relative Elimelech"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Brother" (ahinu) is the legal kinship term used at the gate. PNEUMA's "relative" weakens it.

### Verse 4 -- "uncover your ear" vs. "disclose it to your ear" vs. "bring this to your attention"
- **EMET:** "I-will-uncover your-ear"
- **LOGOS:** "I will disclose it to your ear"
- **PNEUMA:** "I thought I should bring this to your attention"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The idiom egleh oznekha is preserved in accessible form. EMET is too literal; PNEUMA loses the idiom entirely.

### Verse 4 -- "Buy" vs. "Acquire"
- **EMET:** "Buy [it]"
- **LOGOS:** "Acquire it"
- **PNEUMA:** "Buy it"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "Acquire" -- the broader legal sense of qeneh.

### Verse 5 -- "Moabitess" vs. "Moabite" and "wife of the dead" vs. "widow of the dead man"
- **EMET:** "Ruth the-Moabitess, [the]-wife of-the-dead"
- **LOGOS:** "Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of the dead"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead man"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Moabitess" is consistent. "Wife of the dead" preserves the legal formula; PNEUMA's "widow" weakens it.

### Verse 6 -- "ruin" vs. "impair" vs. "jeopardize"
- **EMET:** "lest I-ruin my-[own]-inheritance"
- **LOGOS:** "lest I impair my own inheritance"
- **PNEUMA:** "it would jeopardize my own inheritance"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "impair." Balanced between EMET's stronger "ruin" and PNEUMA's risk-oriented "jeopardize."

### Verse 10 -- "from with his brothers" vs. "from among his brothers" vs. "from among his relatives"
- **EMET:** "from-with his-brothers"
- **LOGOS:** "from among his brothers"
- **PNEUMA:** "from among his relatives"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA changes "brothers" (ahav) to "relatives."

### Verse 10 -- "from the gate of his place" vs. "from the records of his town"
- **EMET:** "from-[the]-gate of-his-place"
- **LOGOS:** "from the gate of his place"
- **PNEUMA:** "from the records of his town"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. The gate is the civic assembly. PNEUMA's "records" is a modernizing interpretation.

### Verse 11 -- "do valiantly" vs. "act worthily" vs. "prosper"
- **EMET:** "do valiantly"
- **LOGOS:** "act worthily"
- **PNEUMA:** "prosper"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew 'aseh hayil preserves the hayil root connection. PNEUMA's "prosper" narrows to material success.

### Verse 11 -- "call a name" vs. "be renowned"
- **EMET:** "call [a]-name in-Beth-lehem"
- **LOGOS:** "be renowned in Bethlehem"
- **PNEUMA:** "be renowned in Bethlehem"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA. The idiom qera' shem means "be famous/renowned."

### Verse 12 -- "seed" vs. "offspring" and tense
- **EMET:** "from the-seed which YHWH will-give"
- **LOGOS:** "through the offspring which the LORD will give"
- **PNEUMA:** "through the offspring the LORD gives you"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Offspring" for zera'. PNEUMA changes future to present tense.

### Verse 13 -- "he went in to her" vs. "He slept with her"
- **EMET:** "he-went-in to-her"
- **LOGOS:** "he went in to her"
- **PNEUMA:** "He slept with her"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the standard biblical euphemism.

### Verse 14 -- go'el: "kinsman-redeemer" vs. "redeemer"
- **EMET:** "kinsman-redeemer"
- **LOGOS:** "kinsman-redeemer"
- **PNEUMA:** "redeemer"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. Full compound maintained.

### Verse 14 -- "may his name be called/renowned" vs. "May this child become famous"
- **EMET:** "may-his-name be-called in-Israel"
- **LOGOS:** "may his name be renowned in Israel"
- **PNEUMA:** "May this child become famous throughout Israel"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA significantly paraphrases.

### Verse 15 -- "restorer of life" vs. "He will renew your life"
- **EMET:** "for-a-restorer of-life"
- **LOGOS:** "a restorer of life"
- **PNEUMA:** "He will renew your life"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew meshiv nefesh is a noun phrase; PNEUMA converts to a verb clause.

### Verse 15 -- "better to you" vs. "worth more to you"
- **EMET:** "better to-you than-seven sons"
- **LOGOS:** "better to you than seven sons"
- **PNEUMA:** "worth more to you than seven sons"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew tovah lakh is "better to you." PNEUMA's "worth more" introduces economic language.

### Verse 16 -- "placed/laid him on her bosom" vs. "cradled him in her arms"
- **EMET:** "she-placed-him in-her-bosom"
- **LOGOS:** "laid him on her bosom"
- **PNEUMA:** "cradled him in her arms"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Hebrew beheyqah means "in/on her bosom." PNEUMA's rendering is interpretive.

### Verse 16 -- "nurse" vs. "guardian"
- **EMET:** "a-nurse"
- **LOGOS:** "his guardian"
- **PNEUMA:** "his nurse"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA with "nurse." The Hebrew omenet means nurse or foster-mother. LOGOS's "guardian" shifts to a legal concept.

### Verses 18-22 -- "begot" vs. "fathered"
- **EMET:** "begot"
- **LOGOS:** "begot"
- **PNEUMA:** "fathered"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "begot" -- the traditional genealogical term preserving the formal register of the toledot formula.

### Verse 20 -- Salmah/Salmon spelling
- **EMET:** "Salmah" (v. 20) / "Salmon" (v. 21) -- preserves LC variant
- **LOGOS:** "Salmon" (both)
- **PNEUMA:** "Salmon" (both)
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "Salmon" in both verses for consistency, noting the Leningrad Codex preserves the variant Salmah in v. 20.

---

*Signed, SOPHIA*
